sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 30-year-old male, has been experiencing pain in the right flank radiating to the groin for the past two weeks.
He describes the pain as being under control but having bouts where it becomes more severe, which is influenced by his fluid intake and physical activity.
He has a history of passing kidney stones, including a couple of instances where he required surgery.
His pain score is currently 1-2 out of 10, but can reach 4 out of 10 during episodes of increased pain.
He denies nausea, vomiting, and fever.
Past medical history: The patient has a family history of kidney stones, with multiple relatives having experienced the condition.
He has a history of passing kidney stones, including instances where he managed to pass them on his own and required surgery.
He is an active individual.
Review of systems: No additional symptoms in other organs are mentioned.
Current medications: The patient is currently taking ibuprofen for pain management.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure is slightly elevated, but other vital signs are not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's abdomen is tender to palpation in the right flank, consistent with the location of the kidney stone.
The patient reports no tenderness in the abdomen, but there is CVA tenderness on the right side, which is attributed to the kidney stone.
Test Results: The patient underwent a CT scan prior to the visit, which showed a 0.5 cm stone located on the proximal right ureter.
There is no evidence of hydronephrosis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a kidney stone, approximately 0.5 cm in size, located on the proximal right ureter, with associated flank pain and CVA tenderness.
Problem list: Kidney stone; active.
Plan:
I will continue the patient on ibuprofen for pain management and prescribe tramadol 50 mg for breakthrough pain.
I will also order a BMP, urinalysis, and urine culture to rule out infection.
The patient will continue to use a strainer when urinating to catch the stone.
I will see the patient again in one to two weeks to reassess symptoms and consider further treatment, including lithotripsy.
Follow-up: I will see the patient in one to two weeks to reassess symptoms and consider further treatment.
